title: Builder's Edge Program Kickoff Call
summary: Erin Stetzer launched her Builder's Edge coaching program, introducing herself, outlining the program structure, and walking participants through the eight core playbooks covering branding, systems, client management, leadership, and business scaling. The session included a live Q&A where Erin addressed real challenges including transitioning from remodeling to custom home building, attracting high-end clients, managing difficult clients, and protecting knowledge through pre-construction agreements.

teachings.explanation: In today's world, a builder's personal identity and company brand are inseparable. Authenticity comes from aligning who you are with how your business presents itself, rather than treating them as separate entities. Clients and partners connect with the real person behind the company.
teachings.quote: Aligning your identity with your brand, because they're one in the same, it's not company and it's not person. It's one thing. And so all of that needs to align for you to really grow your business, because that is authentic.

teachings.concept: Systems as the Foundation of Consistency
teachings.explanation: Repeatable systems are what allow a business to grow, deliver consistent results, and weather difficult circumstances. Systems remove dependence on emotion or daily motivation and create predictable outcomes across projects and team members.
teachings.quote: I'm huge on systems and I'm huge on operations, consistency, showing up regardless of how you feel or think. Those are all systems.

teachings.explanation: Builders work with a large extended team of trades, architects, and designers who are not on payroll but must be led as if they are. The goal is to give these external partners ownership of outcomes so they move toward shared project goals, effectively operating as one large company.
teachings.quote: We're a really lean, efficient team, but in the world of construction or design, we work with lots of other people who are not on our payroll. But the key is giving them ownership so that they're walking towards the goals that we've set.

teachings.explanation: Success in the construction industry requires a commitment to staying in for the long term. Anyone who makes it beyond ten years understands that the business only works if your foundational mindset is that you are in it permanently and will do whatever it takes.
teachings.quote: Anyone in this business over five years and for sure over 10 years, you're in it for the long haul. And that's how this business works for you to make it. Your head is I'm in this for the long haul. I will do what it takes.

teachings.concept: Making the Jump to High-End Clientele
teachings.explanation: Transitioning to top-tier clients is a deliberate leap that requires doing advance relationship work with high-caliber architects and designers, studying their standards, and continuing to take necessary work in the meantime. The jump does not happen passively — it requires sustained effort before the opportunity arrives.
teachings.quote: I identified five architects that I'm going to work with. And I identified five designers and I studied their work. I marched into their offices and said, I want to work with you. I don't know if I'm here yet, but if there's anything, any project you want me to bid on, I'm in.

teachings.concept: Client Intake as a Service-First Interaction
teachings.explanation: The first meeting with any potential client should be entirely focused on how you can help them, not on selling yourself. The goal is to leave them with three clear pieces of information they can act on, which builds trust organically and creates a natural relationship progression toward contract.
teachings.quote: Whenever I meet a client for the first time, whether it's a phone call or a zoom or an in-person meeting, that entire meeting is about how I can walk away and serve them. I have no, this is not about me.

teachings.concept: Distinguishing Edge Cases from Chronic Problems
teachings.explanation: Not every difficult client situation warrants walking away. The key distinction is between clients who are demanding but have legitimate needs versus clients who are genuinely hostile or unethical. Edge cases should be accommodated with adjusted systems; truly toxic clients should be severed regardless of financial cost.
teachings.quote: There's a big difference between wanting factual information and being really mean. And when we get into like the mean evil zone, no way gone. So having said that, now we know good project from bad project.

teachings.explanation: When clients receive unexpectedly large construction administration bills from architects, it can damage trust in the builder. Proactively documenting every architect or designer engagement with a clear reason — such as a plan discrepancy or missing detail — protects the builder, creates transparency, and prevents misunderstandings about who is responsible for costs.
teachings.quote: Every time you engage with an architect or a designer, it's documented and the note should say something like the detail was not clear, or I didn't have a detail to execute, or the dimensions on the plans don't match the dimensions on the elevations.

actionable_advice: Post questions and challenges in the ESH coaching chat on Circle for responses within 24 hours from Erin or her team.
Engage with both the community chat and the coaching chat regularly to learn from other participants' questions and answers.
Before any initial client call, identify three specific things you want the client to walk away with so they can make their next decision.
When transitioning to high-end custom home building, identify five top architects and five top designers, study their work, and proactively approach them offering to bid on projects.
Consider building a spec home to create tangible proof of your quality when transitioning from remodeling to new construction.

Implement a pre-construction agreement — even a simple one from LegalZoom or drafted with ChatGPT — to protect your time and knowledge before a formal contract is signed. Pricing can be based on square footage or deliverables.

When a client's communication style conflicts with your team's workflow, restructure internal roles to absorb the friction rather than forcing a personality clash — and adjust project management fees accordingly.
Document all architect and designer communication during construction with a clear reason for each engagement to protect against client questions about construction administration costs.

When evaluating a difficult client, read their communications multiple times to distinguish between someone who is demanding but reasonable versus someone who is genuinely hostile, and make your decision based on that distinction.
Structure your workday in bookended time blocks for responding to messages and platform activity, rather than being on-call throughout the entire day.
client_challenges: How do you effectively transition from remodeling to full-scale custom home building, and what are the best strategies for attracting high-end clients who value quality over cost?
How do you walk a client from initial contact through to contract signing in a way that sets expectations and manages the relationship from the start?
At what point do you put your foot down on an edge case client situation when it is straining your operations, since it is easy to keep rationalizing that it is just a one-off?
How do you show up effectively in a consulting-during-construction capacity so that the builder and other team members see you as an asset rather than an intrusion?
Before designs are developed and costs are confirmed, how do you lock in a client pre-contract so you are not giving away your knowledge and expertise for free?
What percentage or fee structure is typically appropriate for a pre-construction agreement relative to the scale of the project?
notable_quotes: On staying ahead: 'My whole motto in life is stay in front of everyone. I literally try to stay two to three steps ahead of everyone every day.'

On the construction industry's pace: 'The way that people in this program want to build their company, they want to build their income, they want to grow. But the operations in construction typically moves very slow. It's a dinosaur.'

On having your team's back: 'I will always have my team's back and I will have my client's back. You're not just here to serve the client. Yes, that's part of the equation. But we have our team's back and we have our trades back and everyone can win.'
On project outcomes: 'If we finish a project and there's a winner and a loser, I stink — flat out stink if that happens. So that won't happen.'
On making the leap to high-end work: 'It's about putting yourself two years ahead of the game now and doing the work while you're still feeding your family.'

On pre-construction professionalism: 'We're not doing a good job as builders and general contractors to professionalize how we communicate and operate. One of those things is a pre-construction agreement. Let's just level up and say it how it is.'
